1:
The witnesses meet this famous verse with rare unanimity of embarrassment: as it stands in the Hebrew it will not translate smoothly. Charles Ellicott insists the only possible literal rendering is “Saul was the son of one year (i.e., one year old); he began to reign,” and he judges the numbers in these books “quite untrustworthy”—an old difficulty whose corruption or gap dates from a far-back period, so that the received English “Saul reigned one year” is only a “probable, but conjectural, paraphrase.” He notes that the Septuagint omit the verse altogether, and that the Speaker's Commentary marks the missing figure with a blank: “Saul was—years old when he began to reign.”

John Gill lays out the field of guesses. Some read “the son of a year in his reigning” of a literal infant—Saul's son Ishbosheth, a year old at his accession. Others supply an ellipsis, that Saul himself was thirty or forty. Others take it figuratively of innocence, following the Targum, “as the son of a year, in whom there are no faults, so was Saul when he reigned,” or reckon the year from the day he “was turned into another man” at Ramah, the inauguration being the birthday of his kingdom. Matthew Henry weighs the figurative sense both ways: it may mark Saul as “as innocent and good as a child of a year old,” following the Chaldee, or, more damningly, that he was “ignorant and imprudent, and as unfit for business as a child of a year old”—a reading he finds more “accordant with his character” given what follows. But Henry finally takes the plain construction: Saul reigned one uneventful year, “a year of no action,” and in his second year the events of the chapter unfold.

Adam Clarke would cut the knot editorially, attaching the clause to the previous chapter as a chronological note—these things of 1 Samuel 12 fell in Saul's first year, in which he was “appointed, anointed, and twice confirmed”—and beginning the chapter proper, “When Saul had reigned two years over Israel, he chose him three thousand.” Beneath the textual thicket the moral the witnesses draw is one. That quiet first year, Henry warns, only gave the Philistine “time to prepare for war, and to weaken and to disarm the Israelites”; when men are lifted up in self-sufficiency they are led into folly, and “the chief advantages of the enemies of the church are derived from the misconduct of its professed friends.” The corrupted number opens a chapter whose whole burden is a reign begun in confidence and undone by haste.
2:
Saul's choice of three thousand men out of the great host that had beaten Ammon draws the witnesses into a debate about statesmanship. Charles Ellicott reads it as a stroke of genius: “this is a very important statement, as it tells us of the first beginning of a standing army in Israel.” It was, he says, “the first step towards the development of Israel into a great military power,” the foundation on which David and Solomon would build their conquests. Ellicott defends Saul's dismissal of the larger levy against the charge of timidity—“with true military instinct” Saul saw that Israel was “by no means trained or armed to undertake a regular war” with the Philistines, and so kept a small, disciplined core. John Gill reads the three thousand more modestly, some as “a guard about his person” and the rest “a standing army to preserve the peace of the nation” and “to watch the motions of the Philistines.”

Keil and Delitzsch stand back to note the literary shape of the verse: like the standing formula in the history of the kings—they compare 2 Sam. 5:4, 1 Kgs 14:21, and 2 Kgs 8:26—verse 1 was meant to give Saul's age and regnal years, so that every attempt to fuse it with verse 2 (“he was two years king, then Saul chose 3000”) runs against the uniform historical usage. Adam Clarke adds a tactical guess: Saul divided the three thousand into companies, sending one against Michmash, one against Bethel, one against Gibeah, perhaps hoping by surprising these garrisons “to get swords and spears for his men,” of which, as v. 22 will show, they were destitute.

Ellicott lingers on the one thousand given to Jonathan and stationed near his old home, where the king “would have the benefit of the aid of his family and kinsfolk.” This is the first appearance of “the gallant and chivalrous prince, the story of whose unbroken and romantic friendship with David is one of the most touching episodes of these books,” and Ellicott repeats the Speaker's suggestion that if the narrative was written in David's reign we may see “the effect of David's generous and loving nature in the care taken to give Jonathan his due place of honour.” So the witnesses read the verse two ways at once: a monument to Saul's real and squandered greatness as the father of Israel's army, and the quiet entrance of the son whose valour will soon eclipse his father's caution.
3:
Two exegetical questions govern the verse: where was the garrison Jonathan smote, and what did Saul's trumpet mean? On the first, John Gill and Keil and Delitzsch agree against the old rabbinic reading that would locate it at Gibeah. Gill argues that Geba and Gibeah are “two places”—it “can hardly be thought that Jonathan should place himself… where the Philistines had a garrison”—and identifies Geba with the “hill of God” where a Philistine post had been left after Samuel's victory. K&D fix it geographically: Geba is “not to be confounded with Gibeah, from which it is clearly distinguished in v. 16 as compared with v. 15,” being “the modern Jeba” north-west of Ramah, and the garrison probably the very military post named in 1 Sam. 10:5.

On the nature of the exploit the grammatical witnesses hesitate over a single word. Some, K&D and Charles Ellicott note, render garrison as “pillar,” a sign of Philistine authority; others, with Ewald, take it as a proper name for the officer set to collect tribute; the Targum and Gill's rabbis make it a single governor whom Jonathan slew. Adam Clarke reads the deed as a surprise attack, “for his men had no arms for a regular battle,” and here introduces “this brave and excellent man… a man who bears one of the most amiable characters in the Bible.” Ellicott, gathering the tradition, presents Jonathan as “the perfect type of a warrior,” first in “courage and activity and speed,” whose beauty and swiftness won him among the troops the name “gazelle”—the key, he thinks, to the opening lines of David's lament in 2 Sam. 1:19.

On the trumpet the witnesses divide only in emphasis. Ellicott presses that this was “more than a communication of good news”—Saul “intended it as a summons to Israel to prepare at once for war.” John Wesley softens it to the sending of messengers “to tell them all what Jonathan had done, and how the Philistines were enraged,” and so what necessity there was of gathering for defence. Clarke, taking “let the Hebrews hear”, wonders whether the summons reached especially those “who dwelt beyond Jordan,” called Ibrim from the verb to pass over. Under the debate the witnesses see one thing plainly: a single act of the son has committed the whole nation to a war for which the father is unready, and the blast of the trumpet is at once a boast and an alarm.
4:
The verse turns on two clauses: Saul is credited with Jonathan's deed, and Israel becomes “had in abomination”—literally, made to stink—with the Philistines. On the first, the witnesses agree the credit is official rather than personal. Charles Ellicott explains that “Saul is put for ‘Jonathan,’ though the bold deed had been performed by the young prince, Saul being the general-in-chief,” and that “smitten” implies the garrison was “utterly routed, probably put to the sword.” John Gill agrees it was smitten by Jonathan “yet… by the order of Saul, and so ascribed to him,” and suspects “a concerted thing to fall upon the garrisons… and get them out of their hands”—though he adds the shrewd criticism that it “was not wise for Saul, if he had such a scheme in his head, to disband his large army.”

The second clause exposes a moral wound. John Wesley compresses the whole matter into a phrase: the deed was done by Saul “perhaps contrary to some treaty,” and it is this suspicion of broken faith that makes Israel stink. Ellicott, more historically minded, sets the abomination in the long story of two peoples: “from the first conquest by Joshua” the Philistines “regarded them as interlopers and intruders,” and “between the two peoples there was ceaseless warfare” until the greater kings subdued them—so Jonathan's blow would “at once arouse Philistia.” Adam Clarke reads the same clause forward: “the smiting of this garrison was the commencement of a war, and in effect the shaking off of the Philistine yoke,” and now the people “found that they must stand together, and fight for their lives.”

On the gathering to Gilgal the witnesses register how slow and reluctant it was. Keil and Delitzsch simply record that “the people were called together after Saul to Gilgal” while the Philistines mustered their innumerable host. Ellicott hears in the phrase something ominous: “this seems to have been by no means a ‘levée en masse’… they seem to have come together very slowly, and very quickly again to have dispersed. The hour for a decisive blow was not yet come.” Something, he adds, prevented Saul “from winning popular confidence.” The witnesses thus read the verse as a hinge: a provocation given, a yoke to be broken, and a king around whom the nation will not firmly rally—the human conditions in which the coming trial of Saul's faith at Gilgal becomes so dangerous.
5:
The verse confronts the reader with a number, and the witnesses spend most of their strength on it: thirty thousand chariots against only six thousand horsemen. Adam Clarke states the objection bluntly—“there is no proportion here between the chariots and the cavalry”—and reasons that “the largest armies ever brought into the field, even by mighty emperors, never were furnished with thirty thousand chariots.” He proposes a scribal slip, that sheloshim (thirty) stands for shalosh (three), and notes that the Syriac and Arabic both read “three thousand,” “a fair proportion to the horsemen,” which he judges “most likely to be the true reading.” Charles Ellicott agrees the figures are “perfectly untrustworthy,” observing the rule that in ancient rolls “the number of war chariots is always smaller than that of the horsemen”—here they are four times as many.

Ellicott piles up the comparisons to make the point. Jabin in Judg. 4:3 had nine hundred chariots; Pharaoh pursued Israel with six hundred; David's Syrian foe fielded forty thousand horsemen but only seven hundred chariots; Solomon in 1 Kgs 10:26 possessed but fourteen hundred; Zerah the Ethiopian had three hundred, Shishak twelve hundred. Against all this the probable reading is “300” not 30,000. He allows that Bishop Wordsworth appeals to Josephus, who makes the Philistine force a coalition of nations, but replies that this “would never account for the incredible number of chariots.”

Where the grammatical witnesses labour over the ciphers, John Gill and John Wesley attend to the meaning. Gill reads the muster as defensive and preemptive on Philistia's part: they gathered “to prevent their further encroachments” and to crush a people who now “intended to cast off their yoke.” Wesley, agreeing the huge figure of chariots must be inflated, glosses that “most of them, we may suppose, were carriages for their baggage, not chariots of war, though all their allies were joined with them.” Ellicott closes on the tactical picture the number, whatever its true value, conveys: “while Saul was endeavouring to rally at Gilgal a Hebrew army, Philistia at once, with the aid of foreign allies, took the field,” encamping near Gilgal, “determined once and for all to crush their enemies and their recently-elected daring king.” The witnesses, then, hold two things together without embarrassment: a text whose number they will not defend, and a real and terrifying host whose approach sets the stage for Israel's panic.
6:
Israel, seeing itself “in a strait,” hides in caves, thickets, rocks, high places and pits, and the witnesses read the flight as the visible fruit of guilt and unbelief. John Gill sets the distress against the “vast army… greatly superior to theirs,” so that “nothing but destruction was expected,” and notes Abarbanel's alternative that the last clause describes the Philistines' pressure rather than Israel's dread. Keil and Delitzsch catalogue the hiding-places precisely—caves, thorn-bushes, clefts of the rocks, “fortresses” (the same word as in Judg. 9:46), and pits—and locate the scene at the Gilgal “in the valley of the Jordan,” since the Philistines would “come down” to it. Adam Clarke explains the collapse tactically: the people, “few in number, and totally unarmed as to swords and spears, were terrified at the very numerous and well-appointed army,” and Judea's rocks and caves offered ready shelter.

Charles Ellicott presses the scale of the invasion, citing the tradition in Josephus that “a host of foreign allies had joined”—people “as the sand which is on the sea shore in multitude”—so that “the hearts of the as yet undisciplined Hebrews sank.” He corrects the translation of one hiding-place: the word rendered “high places” is not the usual term for places of sacrifice but “signifies towers,” the same word rendered “a hold” in Judges, perhaps the ruined Canaanite towers still standing in Saul's day.

It is John Wesley who draws out the theology the others imply. The strait came “notwithstanding their former presumption that if they had a king, they should be free from all such straits.” Hereby, he writes, “God intended to teach them the vanity of confidence in men; and that they did not one jot less need the help of God now, than they did when they had no king.” He adds the shrewd note, echoed later by Henry, that “probably they were the more discouraged, because they did not find Samuel with Saul”—for “sooner or later men will be made to see, that God and his prophets are their best friends.” The witnesses converge here: the caves and pits into which Israel crawls are the measure of a nation that demanded a king to save it from fear, and finds the king powerless to still the very fear he was chosen to remove.
7:
The verse completes the picture of dissolution: some Hebrews cross the Jordan into Gad and Gilead, while Saul lingers at Gilgal with a people who follow him “trembling.” The witnesses read the geography as a study in fear. John Gill observes that the fugitives went “as far off as they could from the Philistines,” who lay to the west, and passes on Kimchi's shrewd point that the tribe of Reuben is unmentioned because Reuben lay nearer the danger; the frightened Israelites “chose to go further, thinking themselves there safer.” They fled, that is, to the very region beyond Jordan where, under Saul himself, they had lately triumphed over Ammon—“where they had triumphed they hoped to be sheltered.”

Keil and Delitzsch hold the two halves of the verse together and carry the narrative forward. Some Hebrews crossed over, “as for Saul, he was yet in Gilgal, and all the people followed him trembling”—gathered round him “as leader, trembling or in despair.” Against the topographical commentators who placed this Gilgal on the high ground, K&D argue from the same verb of descent that “the Gilgal mentioned here… must be the Gilgal in the valley of the Jordan,” since the Philistines are said to come down to it. They then read straight on into the crisis: Saul “tarried seven days, according to the set time that Samuel had appointed,” and as Samuel came not “the people were scattered from him”—the melting of the army that will drive Saul to his fatal sacrifice.

Read with the surrounding witnesses, the verse is the low-water mark before the turning of the chapter. The nation that clamoured for a king to “go out before us, and fight our battles” has scattered into caves on the near bank and across the river on the far, and the six hundred who remain do not march but tremble. The trembling is not incidental: it is the atmosphere in which Saul's patience will be tested, for a leader watching his host “scattered from him” day by day feels every hour of the appointed seven days as a pressure toward action. The witnesses let the trembling stand as both the punishment of a faithless people and the temptation of their faithless king—the human weakness that makes obedience at Gilgal so costly and its abandonment so understandable, though not thereby excusable.
8:
Saul waits the seven days Samuel appointed, but “had not patience to wait till the end of the seventh day,” and the witnesses gather to weigh the offence. Matthew Henry anatomises it most fully. Saul's sin was double—“he presumed to offer sacrifice without Samuel… though he was neither priest nor prophet,” and he “determined to engage the Philistines without Samuel's directions.” Henry piles up the aggravations: Saul sent no messenger to the prophet though he had swift men enough; when Samuel came he “rather seemed to boast… than to repent,” going out “to bless him” as a fellow-sacrificer; he charged Samuel with breaking his word, “Thou camest not within the days appointed,” though the seventh day was not yet spent—“thus the scoffers of the latter days think the promise of Christ's coming is broken, because he does not come in their time.”

Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott wrestle with the chronology of the “set time.” Both look back to 1 Sam. 10:8 and judge that the original charge at Ramah must have been “repeated, probably more than once,” for this very occasion. Clarke sharpens the theological structure of the sin: Saul “took the whole business into his own hand, and acted the parts of prophet, priest, and king,” attempting “a most essential change in the Israelitish constitution,” in which “the king, the prophet, and the priest, are in their nature perfectly distinct.” Ellicott locates the sin further in: “his faith failed him under pressure at the last,” and he “acted on his own responsibility, quite irrespective of the positive command of God,” while “the panic… was every hour thinning the host.” John Wesley adds the small but exculpating grammatical point that it was “not seven complete days; for the last day was not finished.”

Beneath their differences of stress the witnesses land together on the heart of the matter. It is not the ritual irregularity alone that condemns Saul; Henry insists “it is not sinning that ruins men, but sinning and not repenting.” Saul “would have this act of disobedience pass” for prudence and for piety, covering “his disobedience to God's command with a pretence of concern for God's favour”—and even his confession, “I forced myself,” became a boast that he had “broken through his convictions.” Henry ends where the whole chapter aims, at the cross that Saul's counterfeit offering cannot reach: “may we never, like Saul, bring our poor offerings… without looking to thy precious, thy all-sufficient sacrifice.” The witnesses see in Saul's two or three hours' impatience the whole disease of a religion that trusts its own performances more than the God it pretends to serve.
9:
“Bring hither a burnt offering to me, and peace offerings”—and the witnesses divide sharply over what exactly Saul did with his own hands. John Gill reads the moment psychologically: Saul spoke “being impatient, and seeing the people deserting him apace, and unwilling to engage in a battle without first sacrificing to God, and imploring his help.” Adam Clarke takes the offering itself to be the outrage: “this was most perfectly unconstitutional; he had no authority to offer, or cause to be offered, any of the Lord's sacrifices.”

Against this the grammatical witnesses enter a careful demurral. Keil and Delitzsch insist the Hebrew “does not imply that Saul offered the sacrifice with his own hand, i.e., that he performed the priestly function,” for “the co-operation of the priests… is taken for granted,” exactly as in the sacrifices credited to David and Solomon in 2 Sam. 24:25 and 1 Kgs 8:63. Charles Ellicott presses the same point as a correction of the common view: “it has been supposed by many that the greatness of the sin of Saul consisted in his offering sacrifice with his own hand, but not a hint of this is anywhere given us.” It is “more than probable” that the sacrifice was performed “by the hand of Ahiah the priest, who, no doubt, was in attendance,” so that “no unlawful assumption of priestly functions, as in the case of King Uzziah” in 2 Chron. 26:18, is anywhere charged on Saul.

The two readings are not idle. If Clarke and the older tradition are right, Saul's guilt lies chiefly in trespassing on the priest's office, a Uzziah-like presumption; if K&D and Ellicott are right, the ceremony was regular in its performance, and the whole weight of the sin falls on its timing—on Saul's refusal to wait out the appointed days and to await the word of the Lord through Samuel. The grammatical witnesses thereby throw the reader's attention exactly where Samuel will throw it in the verses to come: not “thou hast usurped the altar” but “thou hast not kept the commandment of the Lord thy God.” Even Gill's brief note, catching Saul “unwilling to engage in a battle without first sacrificing,” shows the tragedy: the king's fault is not irreligion but a religion that will keep every form while breaking the one command by which his obedience was to be tried.
10:
The timing is exquisite and the witnesses feel it: “as soon as he had made an end of offering the burnt offering, behold, Samuel came.” John Gill catches the narrow margin from the other side, noting Samuel arrived “before he could offer the peace offerings”—Saul's haste had not even completed its own sequence when the prophet appeared. Adam Clarke turns the coincidence into a lament over rashness: “Samuel was punctual to his appointment; one hour longer of delay would have prevented every evil, and by it no good would have been lost.” His cry sums the tragedy of the chapter—“how often are the effects of precipitation fatal!”

Keil and Delitzsch supply the exchange that the verse begins: Samuel came and said “What hast thou done?” and Saul answered that “when I saw that the people were scattered… and thou camest not at the time appointed, and the Philistines were assembled at Michmash, I thought the Philistines will come down to me to Gilgal now… before I have entreated the face of Jehovah; and I overcame myself, and offered the burnt-offering.” They note the very phrase “entreated the face of Jehovah” echoes Exod. 32:11, the language of true intercession that Saul claims but does not truly render.

Charles Ellicott reads Saul's going out to meet Samuel as a window into his heart. It was “the seventh day, according to the solemn injunction… but Saul, in his impatience, had not waited till the end of the day.” Yet the king still “went out to meet him thus publicly,” and Ellicott sees in this “the reverence which the king, in spite of his disobedience, felt for Samuel.” That reverence, he observes, was no passing mood but a “deep feeling… for the great prophet to whom he felt he owed so much,” one that “existed on Saul's part all the days of Samuel's life, and… even after Samuel's death.” The witnesses thus hold together the pathos and the fault. Saul is not an unbeliever storming the altar; he is a man who reveres the prophet, hurries out to greet him, and yet has just, by a single act of impatience, forfeited the thing he most wants. The knife-edge margin—“as soon as he had made an end… behold, Samuel came”—is the measure of how little patience would have cost him and how much its want will now exact.
11:
“What hast thou done?”—and Saul answers with three excuses, which the witnesses examine one by one. Adam Clarke lists them: “the people were fast leaving his standard”; “Samuel did not come at the time”—though Clarke marks the Hebrew lemoed to show that Samuel failed only at “the very commencement of the time… but within that time he did come”; and “the Philistines were coming fast upon him.” His verdict is severe and simple: “Saul should have waited out the time; and at all events he should not have gone contrary to the counsel of the Lord.” John Gill reads Samuel's question as pastoral strategy, “to bring him to a confession,” for the prophet already knew by “the prophetic spirit” what had been done. John Wesley quietly grants Saul the fact behind excuse two—“when the seventh day was come, and a good part of it past, whence I concluded thou wouldst not come”—while leaving the disobedience untouched.

Keil and Delitzsch attend to the grammar of the doom that this exchange sets up, showing how Samuel's “now would the Lord have established thy kingdom… but now thy sovereignty shall not continue” must be read as two suppressed conditionals—had you obeyed, it would have stood; since you did not, it shall not—the tenses themselves carrying the whole tragedy of a forfeited “for ever.”

Charles Ellicott reaches for the deepest reading of the excuses. Saul “plainly told Samuel that though he would gratefully receive any help… still, in an emergency… he preferred to act alone, and, if necessary, to go into battle without Divine consecration and blessing.” In doing so, Ellicott charges, “he must… either have forgotten or disbelieved the story of the Joshua conquest, and of the signal deliverances under the hero Judges,” when the “Glorious Arm” won splendid successes “in the face of enormous odds through the intervention of no mortal aid.” Saul “might have been, and was, a valiant and skilful general, but was no fitting Viceroy of the invisible King in heaven,” who required “before all things the most ardent unquestioning faith.” The twice-repeated charge “thou hast not kept the commandment of the Lord,” uncontradicted by Saul, shows the whole transaction to be “an act of overt rebellion against the will of the Eternal.” The witnesses agree: excuses that are true in every particular—the people did scatter, the hour did pass, the Philistines did threaten—cannot cover a heart that trusted its own prudence above the plain command of God.
12:
The verse is Saul's own summary of his reasoning—“The Philistines will come down now upon me to Gilgal, and I have not made supplication unto the Lord: I forced myself therefore, and offered a burnt offering”—and though the witnesses here are few, they seize on the two revealing phrases. John Gill notes only that Saul “said… that is, within himself,” catching that this is the inward deliberation Saul now lays bare, the private calculation by which he talked himself past the command.

John Wesley lifts a small doctrinal observation out of the first half: “thence it appears, that sacrifices were accompanied with solemn prayers,” for Saul equates having sacrificed with having “made supplication unto the Lord.” The burnt offering was never a bare rite but the appointed accompaniment of entreaty; Saul's anxiety was precisely that he would meet the Philistines “before I have said my prayers,” as Henry paraphrases the same fear elsewhere. On the second phrase Wesley is blunt and exact: “forced myself—I did it against my own mind and inclination.”

Adam Clarke fastens on that same confession as the hinge of Saul's guilt and, oddly, of his sincerity. “I forced myself—it was with great reluctance that I did what I did.” Clarke will not call Saul a hypocrite here: “in all this Saul was sincere, but he was rash, and regardless of the precept of the Lord.” And he identifies the precise command overridden: part of God's charge was “that the Lord should tell him what he should do,” and “without this information, in an affair under the immediate cognizance of God, he should have taken no step.” Here the witnesses locate the tragedy exactly. Saul's “I forced myself” is not the language of a scoffer but of a conscience overruled—he knew the offering was wrong and did it anyway, boasting almost that he had mastered his own scruple. That a man could break “the light of his own conscience,” could pray his way into disobedience and call reluctance a virtue, is for the witnesses the sharpest warning in the chapter: sincerity is no shield when it is sincerity in setting aside a plain word of God, and the piety that “forces” itself past conscience has already forfeited the thing it grasps at.
13:
Samuel's verdict falls: “Thou hast done foolishly.” The chief witness here, John Gill, is careful to say precisely what the folly was and was not. Saul is “not… charged” with “intruding himself into the priest's office,” Gill insists, but with “not waiting the full time till Samuel came”—a fault that “showed his impatience, disregard to Samuel, and distrust of God.” The point matters, for it locates the sin exactly where the grammatical witnesses located it at v. 9: not in usurped ritual but in an unbelieving heart that would not wait on the word of the Lord. And though Saul “thought he had acted wisely, and taken the proper precautions,” he had “acted foolishly”; Gill notes the boldness of the prophet, who “though a king” stood before him, “spared not to tell him so” in the name of the Lord.

John Wesley turns to the promise Saul has thrown away—“the Lord would have established thy kingdom upon Israel for ever”—and guards it against misreading. “The phrase, for ever, in scripture often signifies only a long time,” he writes; the promise “had been abundantly verified, if the kingdom had been enjoyed by Saul, and by his son, and by his son's son, after whom the kingdom might have come to Judah.” The word “for ever” does not, then, imply that Saul's line would have displaced the destined kingship of Judah forever; it means a dynasty of enduring, generational stability, such as Saul might have founded and now will not.

Taken together the two witnesses draw the theology of the sentence. The folly Samuel names is not intellectual but spiritual—“distrust of God” dressed as prudence—and it is this that forfeits the kingdom. The verse thereby exposes the standard by which God measures kings: not “those that disobey the commandments of God do foolishly for themselves,” as Henry summarizes the lesson from the neighbouring verses, but the deeper truth that “sin is folly, and the greatest sinners are the greatest fools.” Saul's tragedy is that he was genuinely trying to be wise—careful, devout, decisive—and that this very self-reliant wisdom, set against a plain command, is what the prophet must call folly. The witnesses leave the reader with the sober measure of the throne: a kingdom “for ever” was within reach, and it was lost not by a great crime but by a want of the faith that waits.
14:
“The Lord hath sought him a man after his own heart”—the phrase draws the witnesses at once to David and to the meaning of the words that describe him. Adam Clarke mounts a careful defence against those “infidels” who cite the phrase to ridicule revelation. David was after God's own heart, he argues, “in his strict attention to the law and worship of God”; in “admitting… that God was King in Israel, and that he himself was but his vicegerent”; in “never attempting to alter… the Israelitish constitution”; and in acting, in “all his public official conduct,” according to the divine mind. The phrase, Clarke insists, “is never used” of David's “private or personal moral conduct,” and “it is unfair and wicked to put another meaning on it.” John Wesley agrees, glossing “a man” as “such a man as will fulfil all the desires of his heart, and not oppose them, as thou dost”—the contrast is precisely with Saul's self-willed disobedience.

On the sentence itself the witnesses raise the hard question of its severity and its finality. Charles Ellicott observes that when the doom was spoken, David “could at that time have been but a mere child,” and he insists the threat was conditional: “God's threatenings, like His promises, are conditional. There is no fatalism in the Bible, but a loving discipline for man's recovery.” Behind it, quoting Dean Payne Smith, stands “the Divine foreknowledge and omnipotence,” so that to “the prophetic view” Saul's refusal to repent and “the succession of David were all revealed as accomplished facts.” Wesley presses the same conditionality further: the full and “final, and peremptory sentence” of rejection is “plainly ascribed to another cause” in 1 Sam. 15, and “till that second offence, neither the spirit of the Lord departed from him, nor was David anointed.”

Keil and Delitzsch keep the reckoning sober: after the sentence Saul “mustered the people… about six hundred men,” so that his unseasonable sacrifice “had not even accomplished the object” of holding the army together. And John Gill notes only that the kingdom is to be removed “not in his family, nor in his tribe.” The witnesses answer the charge of harshness with one voice. “Was it not hard, to punish so little a sin so severely?” Wesley asks, and replies: “it was not little: disobedience to an express command, though in a small matter, is a great provocation… there is no little sin, because there is no little god to sin against.” The verse thus sets Saul's fall in its true frame—a throne lost, but under “a loving discipline for man's recovery,” with the door of repentance still, for one chapter more, ajar.
15:
Samuel departs, and Saul is left to count a shrunken army; the witnesses read both the prophet's going and the king's six hundred as measured judgment tempered with mercy. Matthew Henry hears displeasure in the departure—“Saul has set up for himself, and now he is left to himself”—yet finds hope in its direction: by going up to Gibeah of Benjamin, “which was Saul's city,” Samuel “intimated that he had not quite abandoned him,” whether “to do him a kindness another time” or to seek the college of the prophets “to pray for Saul when he did not think fit to pray with him.” Charles Ellicott agrees the breach is real but not final: “Samuel does not as yet break off friendly relations,” for “perhaps the noble old man still hoped that the brilliant and gallant king would recognise his fatal error.” Adam Clarke reads the accompaniment as help, not merely surveillance: though David is destined to the throne, “Saul is not to be removed… during his life; Samuel therefore accompanies him… to give him the requisite help in this conjuncture.”

The muster of “about six hundred men” strikes all the witnesses as the visible arithmetic of judgment. Keil and Delitzsch observe drily that “Saul had not even accomplished the object of his unseasonable sacrifice, namely, to prevent the dispersion of the people”—the very panic he had disobeyed to arrest had swallowed his host anyway. Ellicott sees “as though the Divine punishment had begun already,” and Clarke marvels at the destitution: “the whole of the Israelitish army at this time, and not one sword or spear among them!”

Henry, ranging over the verses that follow, draws the theology home. Israel's disarming—the Philistines' shrewd suppression of the smiths—was itself the fruit of sin: “if they had not been dispirited, they could not have been disarmed, but it was sin that made them naked to their shame.” He faults Saul's negligence in never redressing the grievance and contrasts him with Samuel, who “fought with other artillery; thunder and lightning, in answer to his prayer, were to him instead of sword and spear.” The witnesses let the verse stand as the chapter's turning: the man who “forced himself” to secure his people is left with a remnant of six hundred, disarmed and diminished, “brought low” for their sin—and yet not forsaken, the old prophet still hovering near, still praying, the door of mercy not yet shut.
16:
The verse posts the two armies—Saul and Jonathan in Gibeah (or Geba) of Benjamin, the Philistines encamped at Michmash—and the witnesses split over a place-name and pause over a structural seam. On the name, Charles Ellicott presses a correction: “the English translators wrongly here substitute ‘Gibeah of Benjamin’ for ‘Geba of Benjamin,’ probably led astray by the mention of Gibeah in the preceding verse.” Saul and his son, on his reading, “entrench themselves at Geba, in a strong position at the end of a pass, whence they could watch the movements of the Philistines,” their “small number” forbidding “any idea of an attack.” John Gill reads the verse more simply, noting that Saul and Jonathan “were now joined to their men, on Saul's coming to Gibeah,” after the diminishing muster.

Keil and Delitzsch use the verse to mark a literary hinge. The following account, they explain, “is no doubt connected with the foregoing, so far as the facts are concerned,” since Jonathan's coming heroism “terminated the war for which Saul had entreated the help of God by his sacrifice at Gilgal”; yet “it is not formally connected with it.” Verse 16, they judge, “commences a new section of the history,” in which “the devastating march of the Philistines… and the disarming of the Israelites” are depicted first (through v. 23), before the narrative turns to “the victory of the Israelites through Jonathan's daring” in ch. 14, “to show how the Lord had miraculously helped.”

Read in that frame, the verse is a held breath. Two entrenched camps face each other across the deep wady, the mustered king with his six hundred watching from the pass, the Philistine host secure at Michmash; and the reader, guided by K&D, knows that the story now pauses to survey the desolation—the raiding companies, the vanished smiths, the swordless army—before the God who was slighted at Gilgal shows, through the very prince whose exploit began the war, that victory was never in chariots or numbers but in his own arm. The disagreement over Geba and Gibeah is not idle pedantry: it fixes the strategic geography of a campaign in which a tiny, disarmed remnant, entrenched at a pass, will be delivered not by its king's forced sacrifice but by a faith that dares. The witnesses set the stage and leave it charged with expectancy.
17:
The Philistines, unopposed, loose three raiding companies on the land, and the witnesses read the plundering both geographically and morally. Keil and Delitzsch trace the routes with their usual precision: one company “took the road to Ophrah into the land of Shual,” that is “in a north-easterly direction,” Ophrah being some five Roman miles east of Bethel; “the land of Shual (fox-land) is unknown,” though possibly the “land of Saalim” of 1 Sam. 9:5. Adam Clarke reads the strategy: “the Philistines, finding that the Israelites durst not hazard a battle, divided their army into three bands” to “pillage and destroy the country,” and it was this dispersal that Jonathan would soon “profit by” in the next chapter.

John Gill turns the raid into a rebuke of Israel's misplaced confidence. Saul dared not “come out to fight them, and there being none throughout the land to oppose them,” the spoilers ravaged at will—“of so little use and service was a king to Israel, they were so extremely desirous of.” This exposure, Gill judges, “was suffered, to convince them of their vain confidence in him, and that their trust ought to be in the Lord their God; never was their country more exposed to rapine and violence than now.” The theme is exactly Wesley's on v. 6: the king demanded to escape such straits has left the nation more helpless than before.

Charles Ellicott widens the lens to the whole reversal of Saul's fortunes. The compiler “does not profess to give a detailed account” of these wars; the great Philistine demonstration “had completely cowed the Israelites,” and now “the old raids re-commencing” swept away, as the next verses show, “all the smithies in the south part of the land.” “The fortunes of Saul now reached their lowest ebb,” Ellicott writes, and he borrows Dean Stanley's vivid picture of the king “leaning on his gigantic spear… with his small band, the faithful six hundred,” weeping aloud over his country's misery while “another voice swelled the wild, indignant lament—the voice of Jonathan, his son.” The witnesses thus hold the raid at two depths: a real military catastrophe, three columns burning through Benjamin unchecked, and a providential humbling, God letting the coveted king prove useless that Israel might learn afresh where deliverance truly lies—and might be made ready for the son whose faith is about to turn the lament into a shout.
18:
The verse completes the map of the raiders: a second company “turned the way to Beth-horon,” and a third “to the way of the border that looketh to the valley of Zeboim toward the wilderness.” The witnesses here are chiefly the topographers, who use the three routes to fix the geography of the campaign and to begin the account of Israel's helplessness. John Gill places the second band's road westward—Beth-horon being “of which name there were two cities, the upper and nether, and both in the tribe of Ephraim”—lying “northwest from the camp of the Philistines at Michmash, eight miles from it, according to Bunting.”

Keil and Delitzsch read the three directions together as a deliberate fanning-out—north-east to Ophrah, west to Beth-horon, and a third “toward the valley of Zeboim toward the wilderness”—so that the whole surrounding country lay open to spoil. And they use the verse as the doorway into the chapter's next theme, quoting the following clause: “The Israelites could not offer a successful resistance to these devastating raids, as there was no smith to be found in the whole land.” The Philistines, they explain, reasoning “lest the Hebrews make them swords or spears,” had, “when they proceeded to occupy the land” as described earlier in the chapter, “disarmed the people… as far as they penetrated, and carried off the smiths,” so that all Israel was reduced to going down to the Philistines even “to sharpen his edge-tool, and his ploughshare, and his axe, and his chopper.”

The verse, thin in commentary, is rich in strategic pathos, and the witnesses let the geography preach. Three armed columns radiate unopposed from Michmash across the heart of Benjamin and Ephraim, while the people whose land they burn cannot so much as forge a spear to meet them; the very routes named—toward Beth-horon, toward the wilderness border of Zeboim—trace the reach of an enemy that moves at will through a kingdom that demanded a king precisely to prevent such humiliation. K&D's careful joining of this verse to the note about the vanished smiths shows the point: the raids and the disarming are one calamity, the second explaining why the first met no resistance. The witnesses set before the reader a picture of total exposure—a nation stripped, encircled, and silent—against which the solitary daring of Jonathan in the next chapter will shine all the brighter as the work not of arms but of God.
19:
“Now there was no smith found throughout all the land of Israel”—and the witnesses read the disarming as a cunning and historically familiar instrument of subjugation. John Gill states the policy plainly: the Philistines, “when they ruled over them,” had “removed” the smiths “into their own country, and forbid any to learn or exercise that trade in Israel.” Adam Clarke supplies the parallels that make the tactic intelligible: “it is very likely that in the former wars the Philistines carried away all the smiths from Israel, as Porsenna did in the peace which he granted to the Romans,” permitting no iron to be forged “except for the purposes of agriculture.” He adds that “the Chaldeans did the same to the Jews in the time of Nebuchadnezzar,” carrying away “all the artificers” as in 2 Kgs 24:14 and Jer. 24:1, and that Cyrus so treated the Lydians.

Charles Ellicott qualifies the sweeping statement with a historian's caution. “We must allow a year, perhaps two or three, to have elapsed while ‘Saul and Jonathan… abode in Gibeah,’” during which “the Philistine raids went on unchecked.” And the note about the destroyed smithies “probably only specially refers to the southern districts of Canaan—especially the territory of Benjamin,” whence Saul and Jonathan “drew, no doubt, the majority of their men of war.” The disarming, that is, was thorough where it mattered most, in the very recruiting-ground of the king's army.

The witnesses do not moralize this verse as heavily as some, but its place in the chapter's argument is unmistakable, and Matthew Henry (on the neighbouring verses) speaks for the pastoral sense they all imply: the Philistines were “politic” in this suppression, obliging Israel “to a dependence upon them even for the instruments of husbandry,” while Saul was culpably “impolitic” in never redressing it. Behind the shrewd statecraft the witnesses see the hand of judgment: a people that would not trust the Lord for its deliverance is left unable even to arm itself, so that the coming victory can owe nothing to Israelite iron. The absence of a single smith in all the land is thus more than a military disadvantage; it is the visible token that when Jonathan strikes, and the Philistine camp melts, the glory must belong wholly to God, who saves “not with sword and spear” but by whom he will.
20:
The humiliation is made vivid: “all the Israelites went down to the Philistines” to sharpen “his share, and his coulter, and his axe, and his mattock.” The witnesses attend to where they went and to the pathos of the errand. John Wesley corrects a natural misreading of “went down to the Philistines”: it means “not to the land of the Philistines, but to the stations and garrisons which the Philistines retained in several parts of Israel's land.” Samuel's authority, he notes, had “so far overawed them” that they “durst not give the Israelites much disturbance,” yet in these garrisons “the Philistines kept all the smiths.” John Gill agrees the Israelites were “obliged to go… to their garrisons, where the trade of a smith was exercised.”

Adam Clarke sharpens the indignity by comparison: the Philistines “did not grant them as much as Porsenna did to the Romans; he permitted the people to manufacture the implements of husbandry”—Israel could not even make, but only sharpen, and that under the enemy's hand. Charles Ellicott turns philologist over the tools, noting that the word rendered “coulter” is elsewhere “ploughshares,” as in Isa. 2:4 and Joel 3:10, so that “we cannot now with any precision distinguish” these implements, while “mattock” Jerome renders “hoe,” “probably a kind of heavy hoe, used for turning up the ground.”

Keil and Delitzsch place the verse in its larger irony, reading straight on to the day of battle when “there was neither sword nor spear” save with Saul and Jonathan. They pause over an apparent difficulty—that the recent victory over Ammon in ch. 11 seems to presuppose weapons—and note it is “very easily removed” when the situation is looked at carefully, the disarming having come after, and as revenge for, that season of success. The witnesses let the small domestic picture carry the chapter's whole indictment: a chosen nation trooping down, ploughshare and axe in hand, to beg its oppressor's forge. It is agriculture, not war, that the scene depicts—and that is the point. A people that cannot keep its own tools sharp without its enemy's leave is a people utterly subdued, and the verse measures, in the homeliest terms, how far Israel had fallen and how completely the coming deliverance must be reckoned the Lord's alone.
21:
The verse is a translator's crux, and the witnesses spend themselves on a single obscure Hebrew word. The Authorized Version reads “yet they had a file for the mattocks, and for the coulters, and for the forks, and for the axes.” John Gill takes it so, of Israelites who “would not go to the Philistines, or were not able” and so “kept files by them to sharpen those several instruments”—though he notes the alternative that they went to the Philistines only “when the mouths, or edges… were dull, or blunt.” Adam Clarke traces the word petsirah to a root meaning “to rub hard,” and though he allows the versions differ “very” widely, he judges the English “may be as likely as any,” supposing it “a grindstone” permitted them “to restore the blunted edges of their tridents, axes, and goads.”

Charles Ellicott reports the decisive shift in modern scholarship. The traditional “file” is “supported by the Targum and by many of the great Hebrew commentators—Rashi, for instance,” but “Gesenius and the majority of modern scholars… render the word… by ‘bluntness.’” On that reading the sense becomes: “and there was bluntness of edge to the mattocks,” the clause forming a parenthesis. Ellicott quotes Bunsen to draw the meaning out: the burdensome necessity of going to the Philistines meant “many tools became useless by dulness, so that even these poorer sort of arms did the Israelites not much service at the breaking out of the war.” He records too that the Septuagint read the verse “with considerable changes,” turning it into a note on the price of sharpening—“tools cost three shekels apiece.”

The witnesses, then, cannot recover the verse with certainty, and they say so honestly; but on every construction the picture is the same. Whether Israel kept a few files at home, or trooped to the enemy's forge, or simply endured tools grown blunt for want of any smith, the result was a people going into war with “these poorer sort of arms” dulled and failing. The obscurity of the word only deepens the impression of desolation it records: even the exact means of their disarming is half-lost to memory, as though the very tools of the account had rusted. What survives clearly is the fact the whole chapter has been building—that at the hour of battle Israel stood practically weaponless, its farming implements blunt and its swords non-existent, so that the victory to come could be credited to no forge in Israel but only to the God who needs neither sword nor spear to save.
22:
The chapter's indictment comes to its point: “in the day of battle, there was neither sword nor spear found in the hand of any of the people… but with Saul and with Jonathan… was there found.” The witnesses guard the statement from overstatement while feeling its full weight. Adam Clarke raises the historical puzzle: if Israel had enjoyed “profound peace and undisturbed dominion under Samuel, how is it that they were totally destitute of arms?” His answer is that “the bow and the sling were the principal arms of the Israelites,” for which “they needed no smith,” so that the destitution was of “swords and spears” for close fight; and he sees in the whole scene Israel “brought to as low a state as they were under Eli,” from which they will rise again “by the strong hand of God… principally by means of David.”

John Wesley presses the same qualification. The words “seem restrained to the six hundred that were with Saul and Jonathan,” for “there were no doubt a considerable number of swords and spears among the Israelites, but they generally hid them… from the Philistines.” The Philistines had not attained “so great a power… as wholly to disarm them,” but thought it enough “to prevent the making of new arms,” knowing the old “would shortly be decayed”; and besides, “there were… other arms more common… bows and arrows, and slings and stones.” Charles Ellicott agrees the words “must not be pressed too literally”—the want of arms belonged especially to “the open valleys of Southern Canaan… the districts whence Saul and Jonathan could most easily recruit,” while the Philistines “armed their fighting men to the teeth,” as the description of Goliath “clad in armour” will show.

John Gill simply sets the scene of the reckoning—“when that drew near, and they were called to it, and obliged to fight or flee, or surrender.” Beneath the careful qualifications the witnesses find one great providential meaning. That Saul and Jonathan alone bore sword and spear is not merely a note of poverty; it is the frame in which the next chapter's miracle is set. When a nation stands, at “the day of battle,” with two armed men and six hundred bearing slings and staves against a host “armed to the teeth,” and yet prevails, the arithmetic itself confesses whose the victory is. Ellicott's own summary reaches forward: Israel is at its lowest, and “in this way they are now to rise, principally by means of David.” The swordless day is the dark ground against which the Lord will paint his deliverance, that no flesh—not even a king's forced sacrifice—might glory before him.
23:
The chapter closes on a movement of troops that is really the opening of the next story: “the garrison of the Philistines went out to the passage of Michmash.” The witnesses agree almost entirely on its function—a transition—and differ only on the precise word. Keil and Delitzsch render it “an outpost of the Philistines went out to the pass of Michmash,” a company “pushed forward… to prevent an attack being made by the Israelites upon their camp.” They describe the terrain that will make Jonathan's coming feat so daring: between Geba and Michmash “runs the great deep Wady es Suweinit,” whose sides “form very precipitous walls,” and they cite Robinson's account of a descent so steep that “we were compelled to dismount, while the baggage mules got along with great difficulty.” Charles Ellicott reads it identically as “an introduction to the recital of the heroic deed of Jonathan,” the Philistines “sending forward an armed detachment… beyond the camp of Michmash, as a protection against a surprise.”

John Gill weighs the alternatives the Hebrew allows. According to Jarchi the two hosts “lay on two hills over against each other,” with a valley between, and the garrison “drew nigh to the passage… either with an intention to besiege Gibeah, or to dare Israel to come out and fight”; but Gill judges it “rather… some strong pass between the rocks near Michmash, where the garrison placed themselves to guard… against any sudden surprise.” He records too that the Targum reads “the governor of the Philistines went out,” and Kimchi “the princes or generals of the army,” who might have gone “to mark out a camp” and choose ground “to draw up in a line of battle.”

So the witnesses leave the chapter poised on the edge of the pass. Everything the narrative has assembled—the vast Philistine host, the raiding companies, the vanished smiths, the swordless and diminished six hundred, the entrenched camps facing each other across the deep wady—is now held in suspense by a single advancing outpost. The verse is a held breath, and the witnesses know it: K&D and Ellicott both point beyond it to the fourteenth chapter, where the Lord will save “by many or by few,” and where the son whose exploit began the war will end it. Read at the close of the story of Saul's forfeited kingdom, the moving garrison is more than a picket; it is the hinge on which the whole account turns from the failure of a king who trusted his own prudence to the deliverance God works through a prince who dares to believe that nothing restrains the Lord from saving.
